carrier_arguments: This estimate is based on damages observed and documented during the inspection.

The carrier will pay no more than the actual cash value of the damage until actual repair or replacement is complete. The insured may then make claim for any additional payment according to the Loss Settlement provision within 180 days after the date of loss.
Any upgrades or additional work not included or stated in this estimate is the responsibility of the homeowner.
Haag Engineering report (Dec 19, 2025) confirmed the property was exposed to a derecho on June 20, 2025, with widespread wind gusts over 60 mph and some locations gusting over 100 mph.
Haag found at the Quonset: 20 ridge caps displaced and 24 field shingles damaged by recent wind forces, repairable using standard insert repair techniques.

Haag found at Dwelling 1 (Unit 23): some dented and abraded siding and vertical trim from wind-borne debris; 6 shingles damaged by recent wind forces, repairable using standard insert repair techniques.

Haag found at Dwelling 2 (Unit 22): one west-facing window cracked from wind-borne debris, one vinyl shutter distorted, one west-facing siding panel fractured, and 12 north-facing fractured siding panels (approximately half consistent with debris impact, half consistent with lawn care/BB/pellet impact).
Haag determined that at Dwelling 1, leaks developed prior to kickout flashing installation and were not from wind-created openings.
Multiple steel bins (Units 2, 20, 21, 37) were determined to be total losses from the wind event.

Prior payments have already been made for Unit 2 ($272,691.96), Unit 11 ($22,967.91), Unit 20 ($274,072.38), Unit 21 ($274,072.38), Unit 37 ($12,014.00), Other Structures ($7,850.00), and Contents ($19,841.23).

summary_paragraphs: Please find enclosed the complete forensic documentation package for the property at 972 132nd Ave SE, Hope, ND 58046, USA. Our independent inspection has documented storm-related damage to the Roofing, Siding, Gutters.

The enclosed reports include annotated photography of all identified damage, an Xactimate-format replacement cost estimate, and a forensic causation analysis establishing the relationship between documented weather events and observed damage.
Please do not hesitate to contact us should you require any additional information or wish to schedule a joint inspection.

conclusion_paragraphs: Based on our forensic analysis of 37 documented findings, the property at 972 132nd Ave SE, Hope, ND 58046, USA sustained confirmed storm damage from the June 20, 2025 wind event. The evidence includes a displaced and deformed metal box vent cap demonstrating sufficient wind force to compromise rooftop components, seal failure at flashing-to-shingle interfaces, and documented code deficiencies at critical weatherproofing transitions. The property encompasses both a primary two-story residence with an 11–15 year old architectural laminated comp shingle roof over OSB decking and a Quonset-style curved-roof structure with a 16–20 year old asphalt shingle system over T&G decking — both of which exhibit age-related vulnerability compounded by the confirmed wind forces. Multiple code violations were identified, including the absence of ice and water shield underlayment at eaves on the Quonset structure in violation of IRC R905.1.1, missing kickout flashing at roof-to-wall transitions per IRC R903.2.1, and exposed weather-resistant barrier due to displaced siding panels contrary to IRC R703.11.2.

The confirmed wind damage to the architectural laminated comp shingle systems and associated rooftop components requires full system replacement across all affected roof areas. The aged shingles — ranging from 11 to 20 years old across structures — exhibit deteriorated seal strips that are inherently more susceptible to wind uplift, and manufacturer-matched replacements for weathered shingles of this vintage are not available, precluding spot repair as a viable restoration method. Furthermore, under the ND-adopted IRC, any re-roofing activity triggers mandatory code compliance upgrades including proper ice barrier underlayment at eaves (IRC R905.1.1), drip edge installation at rake and eave edges, and kickout flashing at all roofline-to-wall transitions — all of which are currently absent or undocumented and must be incorporated into the replacement scope.

Based on the documented damage, applicable building codes under the ND-IRC, and industry standards, we recommend full replacement of all roofing systems, underlayment, compromised venting components, flashing assemblies, and associated code-triggered upgrades at 972 132nd Ave SE, Hope, ND 58046, USA. The scope must account for the unique Quonset arch profile, which demands specialized installation techniques due to the curved substrate and accelerated wind exposure at the apex. The carrier's current RCV of $936,363.53 does not adequately address the full extent of documented conditions, including mandatory code-compliance upgrades, the replacement of all seven pipe collar boots requiring condition verification, and the specialized labor associated with the curved-roof structure.

This forensic determination is grounded in methodologies consistent with HAAG Engineering certified inspection protocols, NRCA roofing industry best practices for wind-damage assessment, and the International Residential Code as adopted in North Dakota. The age-adjusted vulnerability analysis, component-level damage documentation, and code-deficiency identification collectively establish the technical basis for full system replacement rather than partial repair. All findings are derived exclusively from field inspection data, photographic evidence, and applicable code requirements specific to this property.
forensic_findings.damage_summary: Initial roofing inspection at 972 132nd Ave SE reveals wind damage to an 11-15 year old architectural asphalt shingle roof over OSB decking. The property is a large two-story residence with stone and vinyl siding. A damaged box vent on the roof surface confirms wind-related component failure. The roof features ice & water shield at eaves. Wind is the confirmed damage type per inspector findings. The age of the roof (11-15 years) combined with wind damage to venting components supports a full replacement claim given the inability to match existing weathered shingles for spot repair. Roof inspection reveals asphalt shingle system with 1 metal box vent showing damage, 7 pipe collars/boots, drip edge present, and no gutter/leafguard damage. The box vent photo shows a metal vent with surrounding shingle exposure and potential seal compromise. Wind damage assessment is limited by incomplete photo documentation — many fields show 'No response' with blank/empty sections. The single documented box vent appears intact structurally but warrants closer evaluation for seal integrity at the flashing-to-shingle interface. Initial inspection of Quonset-style structure at 972 132nd Ave SE reveals an aged asphalt shingle roof (16-20 years) on a curved steel-framed building with T&G decking. Wind damage is the primary loss type from the June 20, 2025 storm event. The unique Quonset arch profile creates extreme wind vulnerability — the curved geometry accelerates airflow over the apex, generating uplift forces far exceeding those on conventional gable/hip roofs. Boxed steel siding is noted per contractor. No ice & water shield at eaves (felt only), drip edge is present. No box vents, no gutter/leafguard damage reported. No ridge cap damage photos were submitted. The combination of 16-20 year aged shingles on a curved substrate with wind damage and no ice & water protection supports full replacement scope. Photos 16-20 document roof ventilation components, valley details, flashing conditions, and satellite equipment across the property. The inspection reveals copper valley material with no valley damage, insulated pipe collars/boots, no damage to reglet/chimney flashing or skylights, but confirmed damage to the satellite dish. Wind damage remains the primary claim type, with accessory damage to the satellite consistent with the June 20, 2025 storm event. Several sections show 'No response' for photos, limiting full forensic assessment of ventilation and flashing components. Siding inspection of boxed steel siding at 972 132nd Ave SE reveals multiple areas of wind-related damage and hail impact across various elevations. Close-up photos document dented, displaced, and compromised steel siding panels with evidence of storm-force wind effects including panel displacement and mechanical separation at joints. The boxed steel profile shows impact damage consistent with the June 20, 2025 storm event. Directional damage patterns and mechanical separation at panel joints confirm wind as the primary loss mechanism, with supplemental hail denting visible on flat panel faces. Initial siding inspection at 972 132nd Ave SE documents tree damage to boxed steel siding on a single-story residential property. The inspection identifies 4 window/door/conduit wraps, 4 hood vents, 0 gable vents, 10 split blocks, 10 light blocks, and 5 service boxes requiring attention. Areas of concern include all roofline-to-wall transitions lacking kickout flashing. The property shows a ranch-style home with attached garage; no repairs have been attempted. The customer is filing an insurance claim for the June 20, 2025 storm event. Full siding replacement is indicated given the scope of damage across multiple elevations and the number of penetration wraps and accessories involved. Wind damage inspection of vinyl-sided residential property at 47.2706°N, 97.7270°W. Multiple close-up photos document wind-driven damage to vinyl siding panels across multiple elevations. Photos reveal cracked, displaced, and mechanically separated siding panels consistent with wind pressure and wind-borne debris impact from the June 20, 2025 storm event. The 12-20 year old vinyl siding shows brittleness from UV degradation, making it highly susceptible to wind damage and impossible to color-match for spot repairs. Trades affected include siding, gutters, and windows. With 3 window/door wraps, 2 hood vents, 6 split blocks, 4 light blocks, and 5 service boxes requiring attention, the scope supports full siding replacement rather than piecemeal repair.

executive_summary: On June 20, 2025, a high-wind storm impacted the property at 972 132nd Ave SE, Hope, ND 58046, USA, causing damage to multiple building systems across the site. The property comprises a large two-story residence clad in stone and vinyl siding with an architectural laminated composition shingle roof over OSB decking, as well as a Quonset-style outbuilding featuring an aged asphalt shingle roof on a curved steel frame with tongue-and-groove decking and boxed steel siding. Our forensic inspection, supported by 17 field photographs, evaluated the roofing, siding, ventilation components, flashing assemblies, and accessory structures for wind-related damage consistent with the reported storm event.

The inspection documented directional wind damage across both the primary residence and the Quonset structure. On the main dwelling, a metal box vent exhibits wind-driven component failure with compromised seal integrity at the flashing-to-shingle interface, and the satellite dish sustained displacement consistent with high-wind loading. The Quonset building's curved roof profile—which accelerates airflow over the apex and generates amplified uplift forces—shows wind damage to its 16–20-year-old shingle system installed without ice and water shield protection. Boxed steel siding panels across multiple elevations display wind-induced displacement, mechanical separation at panel joints, and impact deformation with clear directional patterning consistent with the June 20, 2025 wind event.

Full replacement of both roofing systems and the damaged steel siding is technically warranted based on three converging factors. First, the aged shingle systems—11–15 years on the residence and 16–20 years on the Quonset structure—cannot be spot-repaired to a weathertight condition because replacement shingles cannot achieve a color, granule profile, or weathering match to the existing field, creating differential performance zones that compromise long-term water shedding per IRC Section R905.2 requirements for weather-resistant coverings. Second, the Quonset structure's felt-only underlayment at the eaves fails to meet current IRC Section R905.1.1 standards for ice barrier protection in ND's climate zone, triggering code-compliance upgrades upon any roof replacement scope. Third, the mechanical separation observed in the boxed steel siding panels has compromised the interlocking joint geometry, rendering panel-by-panel repair impracticable without propagating further displacement to adjacent intact sections.

The carrier's recognized scope at $936,363.53 does not appear to fully account for several documented damage categories, including the Quonset structure's full roof replacement with code-required ice and water shield underlayment upgrades, the satellite dish replacement, and the full extent of boxed steel siding panel replacement necessitated by wind-driven mechanical separation across multiple elevations. Our forensic documentation identifies material and labor scope items beyond the carrier's approved amount, and we recommend a detailed line-item reconciliation against the physical evidence and applicable IRC code-compliance triggers presented in this report.

code_citation.requirement: Flashing required at ALL window and door openings per R703.4. Openings must be sealed to prevent water intrusion behind WRB. J-channel/window wraps are the method of compliance for vinyl and aluminum siding.
code_citation.supplement_argument: Window and door flashing is mandatory per R703.4. When siding is replaced, existing window wraps and J-channel are disturbed and must be replaced to maintain the water-resistant seal between the window frame and the weather-resistive barrier. This is not cosmetic — it is the primary defense against water infiltration at openings.
code_citation.title: Window & Door Flashing/Wraps

photo_annotations.p03_01: Front elevation of two-story residence — asphalt shingle roof with stone/vinyl siding, no visible damage from grade.
photo_annotations.p03_02: Damaged box vent on roof — displaced/deformed metal cap confirms wind event impact to rooftop component.
photo_annotations.p03_03: Front elevation overview — architectural asphalt shingles, 11-15 year age, wind damage type confirmed.
photo_annotations.p04_01: Metal box vent on asphalt shingle field — flashing base visible with minor debris at shingle-to-vent junction.
photo_annotations.p04_02: Front elevation of Quonset structure — aged asphalt shingles on curved arch, peeling paint on steel siding.
photo_annotations.p04_03: Same front elevation of Quonset — wind-vulnerable curved profile with aged asphalt shingles on T&G decking.
photo_annotations.p05_01: Boxed steel siding panel shows hail dent and wind-displaced joint separation at upper course — storm damage confirmed.
photo_annotations.p05_02: Steel siding panel at corner trim shows mechanical separation at joint — wind displacement evident at seam.
photo_annotations.p05_03: Boxed steel panel face shows circular hail dent with coating disruption — impact damage on flat face.
photo_annotations.p06_01: Steel siding corner detail shows wind-displaced panel with gap at trim junction — recent storm event.
photo_annotations.p06_02: Multiple boxed steel panels show directional impact dents and joint separation — wind-driven damage pattern.
photo_annotations.p06_03: Front elevation overview — white steel siding on ranch home with attached garage; tree damage assessment pending.
photo_annotations.p07_01: Wind-displaced vinyl siding panel at soffit junction — mechanical separation at lock confirms wind uplift.
photo_annotations.p07_02: Cracked vinyl panel near window trim — brittle fracture pattern consistent with wind-borne debris impact.
photo_annotations.p07_03: Wind-lifted vinyl siding panel exposing underlayment — lock disengaged from wind pressure event.
photo_annotations.p08_01: Displaced vinyl panel at window wrap — separation at J-channel confirms directional wind force.
photo_annotations.p08_02: Vinyl siding panel buckled and unseated from wind pressure — panel cannot be re-locked without replacement.

photo_integrity.stamp: PHOTO INTEGRITY VERIFIED — 100% | 20 photos analyzed, 0 flagged for manipulation indicators | DumbRoof.AI Fraud Detection Engine
photo_integrity.summary: All 20 photos and inspection form entries pass integrity review. The inspection documents two structures at the same property (972 132nd Ave SE): a large two-story residential home and a Quonset-style outbuilding/barn. GPS coordinates for both structures are consistent with being on the same rural property (within ~0.0005° of each other). Timestamps follow a logical inspection sequence — the outbuilding was photographed first (11:51 AM), then the main home (1:42 PM), with roof-level box vent photo taken at 2:40 PM. The slight GPS shift between ground-level and roof-level photos on the main home is expected. Building characteristics (decking types, ages, ventilation configurations) are appropriate for each structure type. Damage type (wind) is consistent across both structures, which is expected for a single weather event affecting one property. No evidence of staged damage, photo manipulation, digital editing, property misrepresentation, or recycled imagery was detected.

noaa.episode_narrative: Multi-day storm window scan: [2025-06-18] During the evening of Friday, June 20th into the early morning hours of June 21st, much of the region was affected by severe weather. Supercell thunderstorms developed across southeast North Dakota ahead of an approaching convective complex, producing multiple strong, destructive, and fatal tornadoes. This was followed by a powerful line of severe thunderstorms categorized as a derecho, which tracked across much of the region resulting in widespread straight line wind damage. After the derecho passed through the region, there was a period of prolonged destructive winds that affected portions of central and east central North Dakota, as well as adjacent portions of Minnesota. This was a unique event due to the combination of the violent tornadoes ahead of the derecho, and the long duration of significant winds that followed the derecho. Based on estimates provided by North Dakota Agriculture Commissioner Doug Goehring, the state lost anywhere from 50 to 80 million bushels of grain storage capacity from these storms. | [2025-06-19] During the evening of Friday, June 20th into the early morning hours of June 21st, much of the region was affected by severe weather. weather.storm_description: Multi-day storm window scan: [2025-06-18] During the evening of Friday, June 20th into the early morning hours of June 21st, much of the region was affected by severe weather. Supercell thunderstorms developed across southeast North Dakota ahead of an approaching convective complex, producing multiple strong, destructive, and fatal tornadoes. This was followed by a powerful line of severe thunderstorms categorized as a derecho, which tracked across much of the region resulting in widespread straight line wind damage. After the derecho passed through the region, there was a period of prolonged destructive winds that affected portions of central and east central North Dakota, as well as adjacent portions of Minnesota. This was a unique event due to the combination of the violent tornadoes ahead of the derecho, and the long duration of significant winds that followed the derecho. Based on estimates provided by North Dakota Agriculture Commissioner Doug Goehring, the state lost anywhere from 50 to 80 million bushels of grain storage capacity from these storms. | [2025-06-19] During the evening of Friday, June 20th into the early morning hours of June 21st, much of the region was affected by severe weather. Supercell thunderstorms developed across southeast North Dakota ahead of an approaching convective complex, producing multiple strong, destructive, and fatal tornadoes. This was followed by a powerful line of severe thunderstorms categorized as a derecho, which tracked across much of the region resulting in widespread straight line wind damage. After the derecho passed through the region, there was a period of prolonged destructive winds that affected portions of central and east central North Dakota, as well as adjacent portions of Minnesota. This was a unique event due to the combination of the violent tornadoes ahead of the derecho, and the long duration of significant winds that followed the derecho.
